meta._text:
[00:00:08 - 00:00:45] Speaker 6
Okay, good morning everybody and welcome to the March 25th meeting of the Metropolitan King County Council's Budget and Fiscal Management Committee. My name is Rod Dembowski, privileged to chair the committee this year. I am joined on the dais by our vice chair, Councilmember Barone. as well as Councilmember Lewis, and we are joined online by Councilmembers Mosqueda and Fain, and I think Councilmember Perry is there, and I think we will be expecting, I think that is a full committee, so we have got everyone here. I am doing your job, are not I, Gabby? Why do not we start off with a roll call?

[00:00:45 - 00:00:47] Speaker 4
Thank you, Chair. Councilmember Barron?

[00:00:47 - 00:00:47] Speaker 6
Here.

[00:00:48 - 00:00:49] Speaker 4
Councilmember Fain?

[00:00:50 - 00:00:50] Speaker 7
Here.

[00:00:51 - 00:00:51] Speaker 4
Councilmember Lewis?

[00:00:52 - 00:00:52] Speaker 2
Here.

[00:00:53 - 00:00:54] Speaker 4
Councilmember Mosqueda?

[00:00:54 - 00:00:54] Speaker 7
Here.

[00:00:54 - 00:00:58] Speaker 4
Here. Councilmember Perry? Here. Chair Dembowski?

[00:00:58 - 00:01:06] Speaker 6
Here. All right, everybody's here. We will turn now to item three and ask our vice chair, Councilmember Brown, to put the meeting minutes before us.

[00:01:06 - 00:01:08] Speaker 3
I move approval of the minutes of February 25th, 2026.

[00:01:09 - 00:01:20] Speaker 6
All right, all in favor, please say aye. Aye. Anybody opposed? Those are approved. Turning now to public comment, item four. Do we have anybody signed up to offer public comment?

[00:01:20 - 00:01:24] Speaker 1
Chair Demboski, we have no one signed up in person and no one online.

[00:01:24 - 00:01:34] Speaker 6
All right. We have opened public comment. We have nobody signed up. We have closed public comment. We will turn now to our consent agenda and ask our vice chair to put that before us.

[00:01:34 - 00:01:36] Speaker 3
I move that we approve the consent agenda.

[00:01:36 - 00:01:37] Speaker 6
Clerk will please call the roll.

[00:01:38 - 00:01:39] Speaker 4
Thank you, Chair. Council Member Budden?

[00:01:40 - 00:01:40] Speaker 3
Aye.

[00:01:41 - 00:01:54] Speaker 4
Council Member Fain? Aye. Aye. Councilmember Lewis? Aye. Councilmember Mosqueda? Aye. Councilmember Perry? Aye. Chair Demboski? Aye. What is six ayes, zero noes?

[00:01:54 - 00:02:56] Speaker 6
Okay, we have given unanimous due pass recommendation to items five and six. We will advance that to the full council's consent agenda. What kind of schedule? Do you want to expedite it, April? Regular course. Regular course. All right, regular course. Turning now to item seven. Item seven is a briefing. Normally we would have a briefing on Olympia and federal legislative matters in the Committee of the Whole, which is chaired by Councilmember Barone. He and I consulted on this, and his calendar over there was a little more full in the near term, and so he suggested we do it here. Thank you, Council Member Barone. We are joined by Mack Nicholson, our Director of Government Relations for the King County Council. We are not going to get a full overview of what occurred in Olympia, but in particular, there were a number of bills passed and presumably will be signed into law that have significant budget impacts to the county and so we thought we would get those on the radar now for a level setting and opportunity to ask questions and exchange dialogue if people are interested mac thanks for your work this session and welcome

[00:02:56 - 00:10:03] Speaker 10
Thank you, Chair Dembowski. Council members, nice to see you this morning. Thanks for the opportunity to be here to go through the 2026 impacts the county budget. We really see it in two significant pieces of legislation that passed this session. So I will spend a little time going through those. And then at the end of the presentation, a couple of budget items that we saw that that would directly impact county budgets. So the first one would be the millionaire's tax, Senate Bill 6346. This was probably the most significant piece of legislation that passed Olympia. It was the one that the House debated on for twenty four hours running, quite a long discussion about it. The bill itself imposes a 9.9 tax on individuals on the receipt of income exceeding one million starting in calendar year 2028. The revenue generated from that tax is five of it is set aside for Fair Start for Kids Act. It expands revenues used to expand eligibility for the working families tax credit, an expansion of a small business tax credit, and then into the state general fund. Once the tax is up and running, I think the estimate is in between three and four billion a year. That is the expected revenue generation. The bill has a sales tax, essentially a sales tax buy down or an exemption or both really that are created. And these are where we see the impacts to the county. It repeals five thousand eight hundred fourteen and this was a bill from 2025 that extended the sales tax base to cover certain services. This was enacted in 2025. The repeal in this bill is effective 2029 so that sales tax base will contract again in 2029. It also creates a sales tax exemption for diapers, over the counter drugs and grooming or hygiene products, which is also effective January one, 2029. and then a more immediate impact exemption of certain activities from the definition of live presentations that are subject to that sales tax base expansion from 2025, and that is effective this year. The bill also more immediately exempts schools, school districts, and educational service districts from the retail sales tax on certain services, although that impact would not hit the county. So the impacts that we see from this sales tax base contraction, the Department of Revenue in their fiscal note estimated, and you can see there the impact for counties, cities, and special purpose districts. And that is statewide numbers. those are fiscal year 2030 numbers they need to be bumped up just a year so we will see those impacts in twenty nine just because when they did the fiscal note the sales tax base repeal was effective 2030 but it got bumped up a year so those numbers would just we would see the impact a year earlier than what you see there for counties and cities you are looking at about two hundred seventy million combined per year in reduced revenue from that sales tax base contraction In King County, specifically, that estimated tax loss in fiscal year 2029 is ninety three million across all funds. So all funds that get a piece of sales tax revenue. So that would include the local, which I think is general fund, Metro Transit, mental health, safe and stable communities, criminal justice, health through housing, and the cultural access program. King County Metro sales tax is most impacted with an estimated loss of fifty seven million in 2029. It grossed to sixty seven million by 2035. I stole a slide from our forecast presentation because it really shows just in graphical form that loss of sales tax revenue in the out years starting in 2029, which is when that repeal is effective through the out years. And this was in the presentation that O F A did on March 13th in that forecast. So part of our push towards the end of session was to ask the legislature to mitigate that sales tax loss for local governments, for us in particular, for King County. The bill as it started had money in it for public defense for counties that got sort of winnowed down and eventually disappeared from the bill in the House. So we pivoted to making sure that we could be held harmless as much as possible. And ultimately, this was what the legislature did. They included this in the intent section of the millionaire's tax. indicates an intent, a recognition that there is an impact to local government revenues from the sales tax base contraction and expresses an intent to offset some of those impacts by creating a city and county fiscal health account for future transfers from the general fund to mitigate a portion of that revenue loss for local government. So that is the only language in the bill itself that sort of indicates a plan to mitigate some of those sales tax losses. so we have to turn to the budget so this is from the four-year outlook in the state operating budget and you can see there in the four-year total so this would be the twenty seven twenty nine biennium not the current one but the next one the local government fiscal health account and a plan to appropriate two hundred million to that account so that would be an annual number that we would start to see in fiscal year twenty nine and thereafter That two hundred million number does not quite get to the two hundred seventy five or two hundred seventy four impact for cities and counties. So there is not a dollar for dollar sort of mitigation for that sales tax base revenue. It is about seventy five cents on the dollar. So this is kind of the walking through what it all means The legislature indicated their plan to create that mitigation fund for cities and counties. The funding level is expected to be two hundred million a year from the state general fund The distribution formula was not defined we were directed to work with cities and counties to come up with a distribution formula that we would all find fair and So that will be sort of the next body of work ahead We expect we could use Department of Revenue information about sort of that ratio of sales tax that generated from the jurisdiction to come up with a formula That would be fair Again, not full mitigation, about seventy five cents on the dollar. It is flexible dollars available for general use, so they did not put a lot of strings on it. And it is an appropriate amount, so we will need to fight for that amount in every budget. I guess on the plus side, that amount can change in out years. There is nothing really tying it to. It is just whatever the legislature decides to fund through that budget process. So that is the millionaire's tax. The next big piece that provided some revenue, new revenue options.

[00:10:03 - 00:10:06] Speaker 6
Should we give you a break and see if members have questions on there?

[00:10:07 - 00:10:07] Speaker 10
Happy to.

[00:10:10 - 00:10:44] Speaker 3
Thanks, Mac. Thanks, Mac. On the two hundred million, I saw the screenshot that you had that it kind of included this in the second biennium, I guess, in their four-year plan. But that is not any kind of budget bill, right? So there is, I mean, I am just trying to figure out how solid that two hundred million is because you know a current legislature cannot buy enough a future so that this was not enacted into law or anything like that right it is just part of their planning process right it is part of the four-year

[00:10:44 - 00:11:07] Speaker 10
outlook so it was not in the budget bill itself because there is no appropriation in in the current year for that um they put it in that four-year just to try to give us all some uh comfort with the expression of of intent of their plan so would it be accurate to describe it as a pinky promise at this point Maybe a little more solid than a pinky promise, but, yes, that is it.

[00:11:07 - 00:11:13] Speaker 6
I will gladly pay you Tuesday for a hamburger today. Yeah.

[00:11:14 - 00:11:23] Speaker 10
You know, that is, yeah, it is something we will just have to hold them to in the future. So, yeah.

[00:11:24 - 00:11:25] Speaker 6
Okay. Thank you.

[00:11:27 - 00:11:36] Speaker 2
Director Nicholson, can you give us some examples of the live presentations that are exempted from the tax?

[00:11:36 - 00:11:47] Speaker 10
If I did not write them down, I can follow up with you and give you some of those impacts. I do not think I noted those, just the general live presentations, but I can certainly follow up with you after.

[00:11:55 - 00:12:08] Speaker 6
If you are online and want to raise a question, just jump in. I cannot really see you there given I have got a malfunctioning monitor and we have got the slides up. So do not be shy if you are online. I do not see anyone yet or hear anyone. Go ahead, Mac.

[00:12:08 - 00:15:30] Speaker 10
Okay. So the next bill is House Bill two thousand four hundred forty two, and this was the local revenue and fund flexibility bill. This bill started as a Representative Byrd bill. She asked local governments to sort of bring her the menu of ideas we had for revenue and fund flexibility. And so that is how this bill started. A hat tip to Joe McDermott and one of our contract lobbyists who had the idea to bring her the one thousand eight hundred seventy, which was Representative Zahn's bill that granted the nickel authority property tax for public health clinics to include that in House Bill two thousand four hundred forty two, and Representative Berg graciously did. So I will start on the right hand side first. That is the public health clinic property tax piece. It is a nickel per thousand for the operation, maintenance, capital expenses of public health clinics. Counties are eligible and it is a countywide or the countywide nickel levy would generate forty to forty five million annually for those public health clinics. And that is a councilmanic piece. The other new revenue piece of two thousand four hundred forty two is a 0.01 sales tax for children and families. This could begin being collected in January one, 2027. It is also councilmanic, but cities and counties are eligible. The maximum combined rate cannot be greater than 0.01, and so counties have to credit any cities that also, if the county imposes it, would need to credit any cities that also impose it, their full amount. There is no window for counties to go first. It is kind of just a new authority that is accessible to cities and counties at the same time. The revenue is used solely for purposes of providing additional services that assist children and their families. There is some more specificity in the bill as far as what that includes. A new 0.01 sales tax would generate about ten million annually countywide. But as cities also impose it, that amount would shrink for the county and would instead go to cities. um within existing funds there is some additional revenue revenue flexibility provided within the re2 real estate tax revenue can be used for abatement of nuisance properties in the health through housing sales tax revenue can be used for rehabilitation of existing affordable housing operation and maintenance of supportive housing and rental assistance um also for the the state shared sales tax revenue can be used for both new and existing affordable housing units so it is really trying to true up what are a couple different housing revenue streams to make it so that the revenues can be used for both new existing operation maintenance and and sort of rehabilitation side The rental car sales tax, some of that revenue can be used to support criminal justice programs. And then revenue for flood control zone districts can be used to provide flood recovery assistance to households and businesses damaged by flood events and declared by the governor as emergencies. So those are just the main pieces of that bill that provide flexibility for existing revenue streams, not new revenue streams themselves. So moving to some of the specific budget items that we saw in

[00:15:30 - 00:15:40] Speaker 6
Metro. Let us do the same thing. Pause. If folks have questions. Council Member Barone, I think you went down and testified in support of that bill. Do you have comments or questions?

[00:15:40 - 00:15:47] Speaker 3
Well, the first thing, Mac, is that the governor has not signed two thousand four hundred forty two, correct? He has

[00:15:47 - 00:15:51] Speaker 10
not signed it. It is on the schedule to sign at eleven a.m. today. Great.

[00:15:51 - 00:15:51] Speaker 3
Thank you.

[00:15:52 - 00:16:09] Speaker 10
And he has not signed the Millionaire's Tax yet either, or the operating budget for that matter. So twenty days from the end of session. So I think that puts us April three, roughly is when that window would run out and the governor would need to sign or veto by then. But yeah, two thousand four hundred forty two is on the schedule for today. Thank you.

[00:16:11 - 00:16:16] Speaker 6
And you and I sent a letter yesterday asking him to sign it in whole, right?

[00:16:16 - 00:16:17] Speaker 10
In whole.

[00:16:20 - 00:16:27] Speaker 6
helping get that out. Other questions or comments on that piece? I do not see anyone. That is okay. Go ahead, Mac.

[00:16:29 - 00:19:13] Speaker 10
So into the sum of specifics, in King County Metro, there was ten million in transportation budget to fund South Annex Base electrification. That project was put on hold or delayed or I forget what the appropriate term is. But that ten million was shifted to Central Base campus electrification. So we did not lose the money. The legislature shifted it for us so we can use it at Central Base. There was twelve million in the transportation budget for Revive I-five mitigation. This is from a federal fund exchange with the county. So kind of new revenue, kind of not. We are trading revenue from the feds for more flexible revenue from the state through the transportation budget. And then 3.12 million to Metro for a pilot project to provide free transit to community and technical college students. So that is a project that the transportation budget funds to Metro. In public health, that is kind of another place we see That same bill, five thousand eight hundred fourteen, was a pretty comprehensive tax bill from 2025. It redirected some of the vapor tax revenue to the state general fund, which resulted in a twenty one million reduction in foundational public health funding across the state. So that would include funding for the state and then all the local health jurisdictions, including Public Health King County. There was an effort throughout session to backfill that hole, but ultimately they did not. They fell short. So that hole exists, and we will start to see the impacts in the coming year when that decreased foundational public health money would otherwise be available and is not. There was also two hundred ninety two,zero increased in funding for King County Public Health for an airport air quality and asthma project. I think that is ongoing work, and there was an expansion of it along with some funds. So that is really the direct impacts that we see. There were a lot of other budgetary impacts, too. services provided in King County through nonprofit partners, particularly in the behavioral health space, but not a direct impact to county revenues. So did not flag him for this. We are waiting on the governor's signature for the millionaire's tax for two thousand four hundred forty two, which should come today, and then the operating capital and transportation budgets twenty days from the end of session, excluding Sunday. So that puts us April, I think April three or 4. So that is kind of the overview of the impacts that we see from the legislative session on county budget.

[00:19:14 - 00:19:20] Speaker 6
Okay, very good. That is helpful, Mac. Other questions, comments? Council Member Brown?

[00:19:21 - 00:20:01] Speaker 3
Thank you, Chair. I appreciate the opportunity to have this overview. And I think, you know, Mac, you touched a little bit on this. I think the thing that is disappointing, of course, about this process, and I, of course, understand that our colleagues at the state level had a lot of challenges to confront with their overall budget, but the loss of public defense support is a significant issue, I think, for all of us. Because in some ways, it felt like we were You know we were trying to get help with our budget situation and then we have we did not get the public Defense support we lost some revenue in the future.

[00:20:01 - 00:20:01] Speaker 4
So.

[00:20:05 - 00:21:16] Speaker 3
I am kind of concerned about the prospects going forward, especially in the second biennium, because some of the impacts of the public defense issues are going to really be felt next year. And then the following biennium is when I feel like we are really going to start hitting those issues. So just, you know, concerned about that. And I think that there is still more work ahead on trying to make sure that we figure out a way to address that and have the state help with, you know, new requirements, which ultimately are things that the state through the Supreme Court is requiring of us. So I think more work ahead on that front. We appreciate, of course, that there were some positives, so I do want to acknowledge that there is things like the public health clinic funding, and I kind of want to knock on wood at this point. So there are important revenue tools, but I think that the the challenges particularly in the general fund space i think are going to are going to continue of course because we we still have the limitations we did not really talk about that but the you know the one percent cap of course remains and so that that is going to be continue to be a challenge so i appreciate the the overview but i still see a lot of work ahead on on and figure out our long-term health at the fiscal level

[00:21:17 - 00:22:00] Speaker 10
and councilmember if there is a silver lining to that uh it is that public defense, local public defense became such a discussion point as there was that set aside created specifically in the Millionaire's Tax. And I think in a way that was very educational for the legislature writ large. So there was certainly a recognition of the sort of dire straits that counties are in as far as public defense and trying to comply with those caseload standards. And there was a commitment to continue working on that. Um, so we will continue to work on it. Um, but at least there was, uh, it was sort of at the front of the discussion, um, which is, I guess the, the silver lining. Um, there was a lot of conversations about public defense funding for, for counties.

[00:22:02 - 00:22:02] Speaker 4
Chair.

[00:22:04 - 00:22:06] Speaker 6
Yes. Uh, who is that council member?

[00:22:06 - 00:22:07] Speaker 5
Member Perry.

[00:22:08 - 00:22:13] Speaker 6
Council Member Perry. yes okay go ahead is that okay rhonda okay

[00:22:13 - 00:22:17] Speaker 5
oh no go ahead if rhonda had her name hand up please go ahead

[00:22:17 - 00:22:20] Speaker 6
okay councilmember lewis and then councilmember perry

[00:22:20 - 00:22:20] Speaker 5
Thank you.

[00:22:21 - 00:22:35] Speaker 2
Just a quick question about the airport air quality study. I mean, that is a big deal in our district. Do you happen to know if that study is already underway, or was it waiting for this additional funding before it could begin?

[00:22:35 - 00:22:53] Speaker 10
I believe it is underway, and this is an expansion of it. So I think there was approximately three hundred,zero in the previous budget for that study, and then they added another two hundred ninety two million, so almost double the size of the study. So I do believe that work was already underway, and it is now an expansion of it.

[00:22:55 - 00:22:57] Speaker 6
Okay, thank you. Council Member Perry.

[00:23:01 - 00:23:29] Speaker 5
Sorry, thank you very much. Thanks Mac for the presentation and chair for holding it. I think this is a really important timing for all of these pieces that we are looking at. I have two questions if the chair will indulge that, they are short. Okay, thank you. Mac, we were at something like one hundred fifty seven million cliff coming up for twenty eight-29. With all of this in mind, can you give me a number of where we are at now?

[00:23:31 - 00:23:31] Speaker 10
No.

[00:23:32 - 00:23:48] Speaker 6
We are fortunate to be joined by our chief budget officer, Aaron Ruvart, who is here for another item. But Aaron might be able to come up and we will put him on the spot because I bet that is a number he has at least some sense of. Thanks for being here, Aaron.

[00:23:50 - 00:24:22] Speaker 10
Councilmember, I know the one hundred fifty seven million number was what we had been using originally on the impact to the lost revenue from the sales tax base contraction. And those were sort of early figures as we were trying to educate legislators about that local government impact. And so that was kind of the numbers I think, again, we borrowed from the forecasting folks about that impact. And that was a biennial number that I think was maybe the twenty, twenty six, twenty seven number, so.

[00:24:24 - 00:24:28] Speaker 5
And where are we now given all of this in terms of what we are heading into for twenty eight, twenty nine?

[00:24:24 - 00:24:28] Speaker 5
And where are we now given all of this in terms of what we are heading into for twenty eight, twenty nine?

[00:24:31 - 00:25:00] Speaker 11
So for the record, Aaron Rhubart, Chief Budget Officer from the Executive Office. So to answer the question, which I believe is about general fund, we are looking at about a seventy five million deficit for a 2029 budget at this point, based off of the updated U E F A forecast. In the out years, this is going to be higher than anticipated earlier because of the change in the sales tax. So it really impacts the budget in twenty nine, beginning in 2029. So that is where we really start to see it. But we are seeing about a seventy five million deficit.

[00:25:01 - 00:25:27] Speaker 5
for the twenty eight-twenty nine fiscal year. So could you please just explain it at a fourth grade level? How does the seventy five million deficit for twenty eight-twenty nine compare and contrast with the one hundred fifty seven million deficit language that we were using prior? What is that juxtaposition? Can you just make that really simple for everyone who is listening?

[00:25:34 - 00:25:49] Speaker 11
I think so. I think I am following. So the original estimate of the impact of the sales tax base was one hundred fifty seven million. I think that covered all of the different funds, all the different sales tax. What I am referencing is the seventy five million deficit for the general fund specifically in twenty eight-29.

[00:25:50 - 00:26:01] Speaker 5
Righto, so what was the deficit for the general fund specifically before this? When you say it is gone up, what was it? And it is gone up to seventy five million?

[00:26:01 - 00:26:23] Speaker 11
So our current projection is seventy five million. That incorporates multiple changes, so the change in the sales tax forecast, change in a property tax forecast, change in the year-end fund balance for 2025 for the general fund. So that seventy five million incorporates multiple factors beyond just the change in the sales tax done by the state.

[00:26:26 - 00:26:41] Speaker 5
So to be super clear, we are saying that we are not one hundred fifty seven million shortfall over twenty eight, twenty nine, including all areas. We are a seventy five million shortfall. Is that what you are saying?

[00:26:43 - 00:26:47] Speaker 11
No, I am just speaking to the general fund in particular. Okay,

[00:26:47 - 00:28:23] Speaker 5
so because it is not fair to do numbers on the dais, I would really love to hear back from you what the overall shortfall is. That is what we need to hear, the overall shortfall, including general fund and everything else that was included in that one hundred fifty seven million deficit for twenty eight-twenty nine, so we know what that cliff is, the big picture. can you get that to me offline absolutely Awesome. That is great. Thank you very much. And then Mac, this is a big deal. We did not get what we wanted in several areas. And understandably, the legislators worked really, really hard. I know it was very difficult. And they did some of the things that we needed. But we are still in trouble in terms of our ability to support everything that is needed by our residents. And so we have work to do. And the pinky promise that was referenced, which I love, is not something I trust. You know, they did that with the Growth Management Act for unincorporated roads. And we saw how that went thirty some years later. I would really like to have a plan, your recommendation for how and where we are going to be advocating and how soon and to whom so that we know what we can do to stay on top of this. Because to me it seems like we need a very aggressive plan to address what will certainly be substantial losses to our residents going forward. Can you come back to us with what you think, early estimates of what you think and where you think we should be focusing our efforts?

[00:28:23 - 00:28:27] Speaker 10
Certainly happy to come up with a plan and share that with you all.

[00:28:29 - 00:28:38] Speaker 5
Do you have any initial thoughts short of that coming forward? Do you have any initial thoughts of what we should be doing in advocating?

[00:28:38 - 00:30:15] Speaker 10
Certainly. I think one thing is wait for just a spell to see if the governor signs it, and then undoubtedly this still has a long road to go. It is expected that there will be litigation. and potentially a ballot measure repeal of the past millionaires tax bill. And we do not start to see these impacts until 2029. So I think we have a little bit of time to see how this is going to play out before we are going to have direct impacts. In that time, I think we just continue to educate our legislators, our county legislators. I think we started doing a little bit of that during session when we saw the impact of the sales tax base contraction. There were a lot of discussions we had where legislators did not fully recognize that any time you change the state sales tax, you are also likely hitting the local government sales tax. So there was some eye-opening pieces there that we can do a better job of reaching out to our legislators to explain those impacts, and not just that we had not been collecting money, that we build those into our forecast just like the state does going forward that we use to create our budgets. So education pieces along those lines with our legislators, in particular reaching out to our budget writers to let them know we have some good legislative partners in both the Senate and the House on the budget committees. So keeping that discussion going with them and letting them know. uh the impacts that we are looking at and the expectation that um you know this local fiscal health uh fund um is going to be critical uh going forward

[00:30:16 - 00:30:22] Speaker 5
so thank you very much i really appreciate it thank you chair for the indulgence of the questions thank you

[00:30:22 - 00:30:27] Speaker 6
so much uh councilmember mosqueda then back to vice chair barone

[00:30:29 - 00:33:23] Speaker 7
Thank you, Chair. Thanks, Mac, for the update and all that you did with the team during the legislative session. As you mentioned, we continue to hear that it was a very tough session. with lots of moving dynamics so I appreciate all that was able to get accomplished in such a hard year. I also am thankful for the work that was done by the county to support the public health funding that you mentioned. We are very interested and intent on moving that to the council for consideration because of the way that it can shore up the funding for public health clinics that are you know, desperately in need of a long-term solution and we really are appreciative of Rep. Berg and the ultimate package passing as you noted. The other thing I wanted to add to the list of things that we need to go back and talk to the state legislative members about is perhaps bolstering the sound transit message around the need for the seventy five-year bonding bill. I understand that the presentation to legislative members was not as maybe clear as it could have been. And we need to, I think, as a regional partner with Sound Transit and recognizing we have more Sound Transit members on this committee as well, we need to be really clear with the state legislature about the potential opportunity that the seventy five-year bonding bill has as a crucial funding piece to support the Sound Transit three plan. including creating the spine between Tacoma and Everett and the East link. Um, and ultimately for shovel ready projects like West Seattle, which once it has the go ahead, we are within thirty to ninety days of putting shovels in the ground and being able to start building that. And ultimately it is a critical component for our commitment to get to Ballard. So, um, I know that some of us wear dual hats, but I think as a regional partner to sound transit and our, um, tri-county efforts there. We need to go back next legislative session and really strengthen our messaging and information sharing on the Sound Transit seventy five-year bonding bill. Lastly, you mentioned some of the work around housing. I am wondering if you can just elaborate a little bit more on what we might be able to expect from the Continuum of Care funding. Again, thank you to our lobby team and especially Erin Jajic for all of her work on that. [00:41:45 - 00:45:37] Speaker 9
All right. For the record, Nick Bowman, council central staff, the materials for agenda item number nine begin on page one hundred nineteen of your packet. As the chair just mentioned, proposed ordinance 2026-thirty nine would temporarily expand the allowable uses of the real estate excise tax number two, otherwise known as R E I T two, to include county roadway emergency response projects until March 31st, 2027. For a bit of background, current state law permits R E I T two revenues to be used for capital projects identified in the capital facilities element of the comprehensive plan. Under current county code, however, R E I T two revenues are restricted to parks located in or providing a benefit and open to residents of the unincorporated area of King County. As members are aware, the storms and flooding which occurred in December 2025 caused extensive damage to the county's road and bridge system. The most recent damage estimates from the executive suggest costs of approximately twelve million for over twenty storm recovery projects, though these are initial assessments and they are still ongoing and cost figures may increase. Table one beginning on the bottom of page one hundred twenty provides some details and current cost estimates for those storm recovery projects. On December eight, 2025, King County Executive issued an emergency proclamation which waived competitive procurement requirements to expedite storm-related recovery and restoration efforts. Subsequently, the Executive transmitted and the Council approved motion sixteen thousand nine hundred thirty five, extending the waiver for storm recovery-related efforts. Lastly, on the background information, I just wanted to touch very briefly on the Rhodes structural funding deficit and its impact on Rhodes' ability to respond to emergent needs. As the members have no doubt heard a lot lately, the Rhodes budget is constrained by a structural funding deficit that has resulted in an annual funding gap of over two hundred million. While the effects of this deficit have been felt across the agency, the Rhodes capital program has been hit particularly hard. The adopted Rhodes capital budget for the twenty six-twenty seven biennium is approximately fifty five million, with current financial projections showing dedicated funding for the capital program ceasing completely in 2028. Funding sources for the twenty six-twenty seven Rhodes Capital Program include 23.6 million in grants, 9.9 million in surface water management fee revenues, 14.4 million in R E I T one funds, and 7.2 million in operating fund transfers, land sales, and fund balance. As the majority of the Rhodes Capital Program is comprised of grant funding and swim fee revenues, which are generally restricted to specific purposes, there is limited flexibility in moving these funds around to address emergent needs. All right, moving to the proposal itself. Again, Proposed Ordinance 2026-thirty nine would temporarily expand the allowable uses of R E I T two revenues to include the planning, acquisition, construction, reconstruction, repair, replacement, rehabilitation, or improvement of unincorporated county roads and bridges in response to damages resulting from an emergency proclaimed by the executive. This temporary allowance would expire on March 31st, 2027. If adopted, the executive intends to reallocate an unspecified amount of retude revenues in the first omnibus budget to fund roads-related storm recovery projects. I say unspecified because at the time this staff report was written, the total amount of retude revenues to be reallocated was still being deliberated by the executive, though I believe that total number may have been settled on and executive staff may be able to share that with the committee. While the board's ordinance would not actually reallocate any funding, I think it is important to discuss some budget details to provide context.

[00:45:38 - 00:45:39] Speaker 6
All right. We will do it on a high level. Yeah.

[00:45:42 - 00:46:40] Speaker 9
The roads quick response program is the program which allows roads to respond to emerging needs that require immediate attention. According to the executive, prior to the storm, the quick response project balance was about seven million. This appropriation was expected to cover unplanned roads and bridges work during the full 2026-2027 biennium. Roads leveraged the quick response appropriation authority to address the needs of some bigger projects, including the Bering temporary bridge and Edgewick repairs following the December 2025 flooding event. Given the scale of the damage incurring the storm, roads do down that quick response budget much more quickly than anticipated. If R E I T is allocated to cover the urgent needs of the Bering Temporary Bridge and Edgwick repairs, this would allow roads to restore original quick response budget for the upcoming biennium. Without it, however, there is insufficient balance under one million to address any big or multiple small quick response work in the full biennium. And roads and bridges may have to be closed if repairs cannot be made.
